mappings.rationaleAI: Pythagoras (A.1.a), the Sophists (A.1.b), Parmenides and Eleaticism (A.1.c), and Democritus' Atomism (A.1.d) are all covered.
Theory of Forms maps to Platonism (A.1.e), metaphysical theory (B.1), and conceptions of knowledge through Idealism (C.1).
Logic, metaphysics, and biology map to Aristotelianism (A.1.f) and metaphysical theory (B.1).
Zeno, Cleanthes, and Chrysippus' doctrines map directly to Stoicism (A.1.g).
Epicurus' materialism maps to Epicureanism (A.1.h), Materialism (A.2.d), and Atomism (B.1).
Pyrrhonism maps to Skepticism (A.1.i) and validity of knowledge (C.2).
Aquinas's synthesis maps to Scholasticism (A.1.j) and Thomism (B.1).
Scholastic traditions and mind-body problems map to Scholasticism (A.1.j) and mind-body views (B.4).
Methodological doubt and dualism map to Cartesianism (A.2.a), Rationalism (A.2.c), metaphysics (B.1), and dualism (B.4).
Empiricist theory maps to Empiricism (A.2.b) and foundations of knowledge (C.3).
Empiricism, skeptical challenge, and perception theories map to Empiricism (A.2.b), validity of knowledge (C.2), and foundations (C.3).
Substance monism maps to Rationalism (A.2.c), metaphysical theory (B.1), and views of God (B.2).
Monadology and possible worlds map to Rationalism (A.2.c) and metaphysical theory (B.1).
Transcendental idealism maps to Kantianism (A.2.e) and validity of knowledge (C.2).
Absolute idealism and dialectics map to Idealism (A.2.f) and Hegelianism (A.2.g).
Dialectical materialism maps to Marxism (A.2.j), Materialism (A.2.d), and metaphysical theory (B.1).
Utilitarian theory maps directly to Utilitarianism (A.2.h).
Mill's contributions map to Empiricism (A.2.b) and Utilitarianism (A.2.h).
Authenticity and freedom map to Existentialism (A.2.n), conceptions of man (B.3), and deontological ethics (D.2).
Logical analysis maps directly to Analytic and Linguistic philosophy (A.2.o).

German idealism and rationalism map to Rationalism (A.2.c), Kantianism (A.2.e), and Idealism (A.2.f).

Materialism and empiricism connect to Empiricism (A.2.b) and Materialism (A.2.d).
Subjectivity and individual choice map to Existentialism (A.2.n).

Metaphysics of will connects to Idealism (A.2.f), metaphysical theory (B.1), and mind-body (B.4).
Phenomenological method and Dasein map to Phenomenology (A.2.m), Existentialism (A.2.n), and conceptions of man (B.3).
Tractatus connects to Logical Empiricism (A.2.i); Investigations to Analytic philosophy (A.2.o).
Analytic philosophy maps to Analytic philosophy (A.2.o); perception views to Realism (A.2.k).
Power-knowledge analysis connects to Marxism (A.2.j) and validity of knowledge (C.2).
Deconstruction connects to Phenomenology (A.2.m) and status of the universal (C.4).
Frankfurt School tradition connects to Marxism (A.2.j); rational consensus to Pragmatism (A.2.l).
Frankfurt School critical theory maps to Marxism (A.2.j).
Frankfurt School alienation and reification concepts map to Marxism (A.2.j).
Anti-foundationalism connects to validity of knowledge (C.2) and epistemic status of theories (C.5).
Challenge to fixed meanings connects to validity of knowledge (C.2) and status of the universal (C.4).
Secular humanism connects to views of God (B.2) and conceptions of man (B.3).
Rejection of theistic belief maps to atheism (B.2).

Systematic study of the divine maps to views concerning God (B.2).

Determinism and moral responsibility connect to mind-body (B.4) and deontological ethics (D.2).

Ancient philosophy spans Platonism (A.1.e), Aristotelianism (A.1.f), Stoicism (A.1.g), Epicureanism (A.1.h), and Scepticism (A.1.i).

Covers major metaphysical theories of causation from Aristotle through modern approaches including necessity and physicalism — directly relevant to metaphysical theories and views of the sources of knowledge.
Covers arguments for and against God's existence, faith and reason — directly relevant to views concerning God's existence, attributes, and knowledge.
Covers the ontological argument, perfect-being theology, and medieval Scholastic philosophy — directly relevant to Scholasticism and views concerning God's existence.
Covers realism versus anti-realism, the demarcation problem, and scientific reasoning — directly relevant to Positivism and Logical Empiricism and epistemic status of scientific theories.
Covers philosophical theories of happiness from Mill and subjective versus objective conceptions of the good — relevant to Utilitarianism and metaethical theories (unresolved leaf D.1).

Engages existentialism through Sartre and Nietzsche, linguistic philosophy through Wittgenstein, and materialist critiques through Marx — relevant to Existentialism and conceptions of man as knower and maker.
